observations.recommendation:
We recommended, and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to regularly reconcile their books, including addressing the net discrepancy of ₱ (6.931) million;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the unaccounted discrepancies totaling ₱8.582 million and to promptly record the necessary adjustments for identified reconciling items in the Bank Reconciliation Statements, in order to reflect the correct balance of Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account; and
Ensure compliance with the procedures prescribed in COA Circular No. 2023-008, dated August 15, 2023, particularly in cases where transactions lack supporting documents, and subsequently initiate an investigation to determine the cause of the loss of documents and identify the official or employee responsible.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare a schedule of unreleased checks as of year-end and provide the same to the Municipal Accountant;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the unreleased checks as of year-end to the Cash in Bank-LCCA based on the schedule provided by the Municipal Treasurer for the subsequent years; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a JEV to revert all stale checks identified in the Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to establish a timeline in the processing of disaster-related procurements scheduled in the last quarter of the year and ensure that sufficient time is allotted for the reconciliation and determination of unutilized LDRRMF, which shall be mandatorily transferred to Trust Liability-DRRM prior to the close of the calendar year pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended, and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Ensure that the appropriations for the 20% Development Fund are properly planned, procurement and implementation-ready, with specific identification of the PPAs to be implemented;
Ensure that, in cases of lump sum appropriations containing general terms that cannot be avoided, a separate Sangguniang Bayan authorization is always secured to serve as authority to enter into a contract on behalf of the Municipal Government; and
Direct the Municipal Planning Development Coordinator to strengthen their monitoring controls, particularly for projects charged to the 20% DF that are transferred to the respective barangays to ensure their timely implementation and proper liquidation of transferred funds.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to design and implement more effective collection strategies to improve collections, when necessary, enforce the remedies provided under Sections 256 to 260 of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Instruct the HRMO-designate to enforce the rule on Leave Without Pay (LWOP) for employees who incur absences beyond their available leave credits and to promptly notify the payroll in-charge to ensure proper salary deductions;
Direct the payroll in-charge to deduct from the subsequent salaries of employees who have negative balances in their leave credits balance the total amount of overpayment resulting from non-deduction of their leaves without pay; and
Ensure the strict implementation of the rules on unauthorized absences and tardiness prescribed under CSC Memorandum Circular No. 01, s. 2017, including the imposition of appropriate sanctions, when warranted.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the members of the Local Finance Committee to strictly observe the reportorial and posting requirements relative to the management and utilization of its LGSF-GEF pursuant to DBM LBC No. 159 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2024-021.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant or the personnel in charge of the premium remittances to maintain a monthly monitoring of their schedule of premium contributions to PhilHealth to ensure timely remittance and avoid prolonged delay that may affect employee benefits.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the LGU GFPS to formulate their three-year GAD Agenda to ensure that priority gender issues are properly identified and addressed, and align the annual GAD Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) with the government’s strategic goal of women’s empowerment and gender equality.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the adjusting entries to take-up the cost of the asset in the General Fund books of accounts and to compute the corresponding depreciation in order to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the required adjusting entries to record the cost of the Radio Communication Repeater amounting to ₱402,510.00 in the appropriate PPE account.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel to henceforward, adhere strictly with the laws, rules and regulations in the utilization of funds received from the National Government Agencies.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the required adjusting entries for those PPAs that are still under construction and to recognize the completed projects in the appropriate PPE account in order to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible for the year to be used as the basis in setting up the RPT and SET Receivables.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounting records with the tax due listing of the Municipal Treasurer and make necessary adjustments, where appropriate, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Management to ensure that the appropriations for the 20% Development Fund are properly planned and, procurement and implementation ready.

We also recommended and the Management agreed to ensure that, in cases of lump sum appropriations containing general terms that cannot be avoided, a separate SB authorization always be secured by the Municipal Mayor to serve as authority to enter into a contract on behalf of the Municipal Government.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and the concerned personnel to henceforth, submit the cash advance voucher with complete supporting documents to the Audit Team within seven days after the release of the check.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the following:

Local Finance Committee to prepare and submit the Quarterly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project Implementation using the prescribed format (Annex C) until such time that the Unified Reporting System for LGUs has been developed and can be used by the LGUs; and

Concerned department/personnel to post updates on the Municipality’s website and make the necessary posting upon preparation of the mentioned reports as part of their public disclosure requirement.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure that all prospective suppliers who would like to participate in the procurement process of the Municipality are duly registered with the PhilGEPS in accordance with the requirements of the Revised IRR of the RA No. 9184.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee to request access for alternative methods of procurement from PhilGEPS in order to comply with the publication of RFQs and posting of notices.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the General Services Office to:
a. Facilitate the LTO registration and insurance of all motor vehicles owned by the Municipality;

b.  Refrain from using unregistered vehicles owned by the Municipality and regularly monitor the expiration of all motor vehicles registration and its timely renewal to avoid invalidation, fines/penalties for delinquency and additional losses; and

c.  Conduct physical inventory of all the Motor Vehicles owned by the Municipality and reconcile the result with the accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain a maintenance/ repair record of each equipment/vehicle owned and/or operated by the Municipal Government.

We also recommended that damaged and unserviceable supplies and properties be kept in a safe stockroom located within the premise of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Inventory Committee to spearhead the planning and the conduct of complete physical count of all PPE owned by the Municipality and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team pursuant to the provisions stipulated in COA Circular Nos. 2020-006 and 92-386 and Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGU.

d.) Continue to dispose all PPEs found unserviceable and without any
salvage value; accomplish and submit the IIRUP to the OCA with
complete PPE information such as property number, serial number or
specification to expedite the derecognition of PPEs in the books;
e.) For the missing PPEs, conduct an investigation, submit an investigation
report and/or the demand letter to the OCA for the recognition of
corresponding receivables from personnel concerned; or request from COA
an Authority for Derecognition with prescribed complete supporting
documents for those missing PPEs which accountability/responsibility
could not be pinpointed, as prescribed in COA Circular 2020-006;

2.) City Accountant to:
a. Work together with the CGSO in reconciling the PPELC with the RPCPPE
and update the PPELC in the course of reconciliation; and
b. Take up the necessary accounting entries to derecognize disposed PPEs
based on original copies of the IIRUP and other supporting documents
from the CGSO.

4.33. We recommended the City Mayor to:
1. Require the City Accountant to:
a. Reconcile the Supply Ledger Cards (SLC) and Subsidiary Ledgers of
Inventory Accounts;
b. Record issued supplies and materials based on SSMI prepared by the
CGSO;
c. Make the adjusting entry to recognize the distribution of inventories
costing P3,765,145.00 and to correct the erroneous recording of
distributed inventories costing P963,550.00 ; and
d. Obtain the lacking Monthly Fuel Consumption Report from the CGSO
and make the necessary entries to record the fuel consumed for CY
2023.
2. Direct the Inventory Team to complete the physical count of inventories
as at year-end and periodically conduct physical counts of Inventories
semi-annually, reconciling the results with the accounting records.
3. Require the CSWDO, OCAS and CVO to submit to the CGSO, for the
preparation of SSMI, the utilization report of inventory items received and
issued to end-users/beneficiaries.
4. Require the CGSO to:
a. prepare SSMI duly supported with RIS on issued supplies and
materials and submit to the City Accountant for the recording of
expenditures;
b. Require the fifteen (15) end-users of Fuel, Oil and Lubricants
Inventory to submit the lacking Fuel Consumption Report and submit
the report to OCA for the recognition of expenditures; and
c. Prepare and maintain Stock Cards on all inventory items and reconcile
records with the supply ledgers cards of the Municipal Accounting
Office.
5. Require the CHU to submit proof of distribution on drugs and medicines
costing P16,906,487.50 and make a separate record for monitoring of drugs
and medicines received from DOH.

5.6. We recommended that the City Mayor require the Clerk/Personnel-in-Charge
to:
1. Use the prescribed forms such as the Requisition and Issue Voucher,
Summary of Requisition and Issue Voucher and Daily Gas Issue Record for monitoring
the receipt and issuance of the fuel;
2. Ensure that all fuel withdrawals are properly supported with approved
withdrawal slip;
3. Reconcile the Driver’s Trip Ticket with the Monthly Fuel Consumption
Report and Summary of Monthly Report of Official Travels on a monthly basis, as
mandated under COA Circular No. 77-61 to determine the validity and appropriate
fuel usage; and
4. Enforce the “No Driver’s Trip Ticket, No Trip Policy”.

6.6. We recommended that the City Mayor require the following:
a.) City General Services Officer to:
1. Prepare the SSMI and consolidate it with the RIS weekly for supplies and
materials issued.
2. Submit the SSMI together with the RIS to the City Accountant as a
basis for the recognition of expense.
b.) City Accountant to record the appropriate Inventory account of all procured
small items for disaster related activities and recognize the related expenses
through the submitted SSMI and RIS by the GSO as prescribed in the MNGAS,
Volume I and II.

7.12. To ensure that the 20% DF is optimally utilized and monitored, we recommended
that the City Mayor, as Chairman of the City Development Council, require the:
1. City Engineer’s Office, City Planning and Development Office and other
implementing offices, to fast-track and monitor the implementation of identified
PPAs of the 20% DF in order to achieve the objectives of the fund;
2. The City Budget Officer to render periodic updates to the City Mayor on the
SAAOB of the 20% DF and identify excess funds from completed projects and
recommend their reversion and re-appropriation;
3. The City Planning and Development Office to ensure the implementation of
identified PPAs of the 20% DF during the year and recommend to the City
Development Council the re-alignment of appropriation of PPAs funded from its
Continuing Appropriation that are no longer identified as priority development
PPAs or could no longer be implemented due to causes beyond the control of
responsible officials;
4. City Development Council, to revisit PPAs charged to Continuing
Appropriation of 20% DF, and identify balance to be rechanneled to priority and
relevant PPAs that are procurement and implementation-ready.

8.5. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer’s Office, City
Planning and Development Office and other implementing offices concerned to
monitor the implementation City Priority Projects to ensure optimal utilization of
the fund.

8.6. We also recommended that the City Budget Officer identify excess funds from
completed City Priority Projects and recommend their reversion, making
them available for re-appropriation.

9.16. We recommended that the City Mayor require the CDRRMO and the CDRRM
Council to:
a.) Stop the use of LDRRMF for non-disaster related PPAs and submit a written
justification on the use of P3.6M LDRRMF for MOOE and the paid
disbursements of P1.4M fuel expenses for COVID-19 operation program; and
b.) Fully Utilize the LDRRMF by implementing all the identified PPAs in the AIP
and LDRRFMFIP to strengthen and enhance the City’s disaster preparedness,
response capabilities and avoid potential damages during disasters.

10.23. We recommended that the City Mayor:
1. Direct Department Heads, particularly the City Social Welfare and
Development Officer, City Agriculturist and City Engineer, who are in-
charge of projects, programs or activities funded from the fund transfers to
fast track their implementation.
2. To require the City Accountant to:
a) Initiate preparation of Final Utilization Report and refund any unutilized
balance of fund transfers;
b) Secure confirmation with the Source Agencies if submission of
liquidation reports of fund transfers totaling P232,852.09 are no longer
required, otherwise undo the reclassification of these funds as Trust
Liability from the Due to NGAs account;
c) Revert to its original account the unused funds of pooled balances on
Philhealth remittances received in prior for implementation of Philhealth
programs; and
d) Secure confirmation with the Source Agencies if trust receipt totalling
P1,627,563.09 can be recognized as income of the LGU, otherwise, make
the necessary entries to revert back the funds to Due to NGAs Account.
3. Remind the City Budget Officer to ensure that fund transfers is spent only for
the specific purpose for which it was it was created or for which it came into
the possession of the local government unit.
4. Require the City Health Officer to submit the lackings documents to support
the payment of OCA to personnel of CDRRMO, HCI-Office of the City Mayor,
BHW and BNS totalling P2,131,125.00.

11.11. We recommended that the City Mayor:
1. Direct all concerned Department Heads, who are in-charge of the
implementation of projects, programs or activities funded from trust receipts
and booked as Trust Liability Account, to secure the documents identifying the
specific purpose of the unutilized trust receipts and fast track its implementation.
2. Require the City Accountant to revert to its original account the unused funds
of pooled balances on Philhealth remittances received in prior years for
implementation of different Philhealth programs.

12.18. We recommended that the City Mayor, through the City Health Officer to
substantiate the payment of Hazard Pay, in compliance with COA Circular No.
2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 by:
a.) Issuing a Special Order covering the assignment of public health workers to
hazardous/difficult areas;
b.) Securing a Certification by the Secretary of the DOH that the place of
assignment/travel is a disease-infested/distress area; and
c.) Attaching the complete supporting documents to the disbursement vouchers.

12.19. Likewise, we recommended the City Accountant to ensure that disbursement
vouchers are supported with complete documentation and strictly observe the
guidelines in the computation of the hazard pay, subsistence and laundry
allowances.

13.19. We recommended that the City Mayor, as Chairman of the Local Peace and Order
Council to:
1.) Include in the AIP all PPAs that are directly related to crime prevention and law
enforcement activities, capability development for personnel of law
enforcement agencies, volunteers/partners, or programs for anti-illegal drug,
illegal gambling, counter-insurgency, and/or counter-terrorism efforts, among
others that will directly contribute to the maintenance of the peace and order
and public safety;
2.) Refrain from using the appropriations for Peace and Order for purposes other
than those authorized under Joint Circular (JC) No 2015-01 dated January 8,
2015; and
3.) Direct the:
a.) City Planning and Development Coordinator to thoroughly prepare and
review the POPs Plan and AIP of the LGU incorporating therein detailed
list of PPAs and their corresponding appropriation; and
b.) City Accountant to:
1.) require the PNP Personnel to submit the lacking supporting documents
related to the payment of operational support/incentive; and
2.) Henceforth, ensure that the disbursement vouchers are supported with
complete documentation.

14.21. We recommended that City Mayor direct the BAC to validate, verify and
ascertain the veracity of the eligibility documents submitted by interested
bidders to prove compliance with the requirements of the contract and bidding
documents including the Statements of All On-going Projects in determining the
eligibility of the bidder; and

14.22. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to:
1. Consider in the computation of contract time duration the
rainy/unworkable days pre-determined during the conduct of detailed
engineering;
2. Make sure that final acceptance of completed infrastructure projects must be
made one year from project completion;
3. Ensure that all conditions preliminary to the commencement of construction
have been addressed and that a certification shall be executed that detailed
preliminary engineering has been conducted prior to the start of procurement
activities to ensure that necessary requirements and documents have been
undertaken for the project to be completed as planned; and
4. Conduct timely monitoring of actual completion status of infrastructure
projects and send notices to contractors for infrastructure projects with delayed
implementation.; and
5. Accept only retention money and warranty security in the form of Surety Bond
that is properly filled-up with all the necessary data that will conform to the
requirements of the particular project, specifically, the amount, date of issuance
and period of validity.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts in retrieving the records of the abovementioned properties and to provide the corresponding depreciation using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation in compliance to existing government rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to and other officials concerned to allocate expenditures similar to this nature to a separate fund, i.e., Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses, instead of charging the same to the 20% Development Fund.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry to recognize the unrecorded expense as a prior period adjustment amounting to ₱0.815 million.

Lastly, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to henceforward recognize as inventory the supplies and materials for distribution upon receipt and expensed when issued. Likewise, subsequent issuance of the same should be supported with Requisition and Issuance Slip (RIS) in order to properly account for the inventories of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned personnel to submit the list of recipients to support the liquidation on the issuances of the various medical supplies received.

Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to record the purchases of inventories in the appropriate inventory accounts and the consumption thereof as expense, in accordance with IPSAS 12. Likewise, subsequent issuance of the same should be supported with RIS.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to review the previous journal entries made and make necessary adjusting entries to correct the appropriate asset accounts. 

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant agreed to henceforward record the purchases of construction materials as inventory and the payment of labor using the construction in progress account.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:

a.	Post for all procurement through public bidding, the NTPs and contracts/POs in the PhilGEPS website pursuant to RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR to promote transparency and competitiveness in government procurement; and

b.	Request access for alternative methods of procurement from PhilGEPS in order to comply with the publication of RFQs and posting of notices.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee and other officials concerned to award contracts within the obligatory 3-month period pursuant to Section 38 of the Revised IRR of the RA No. 9184.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the LDRRMO to review and update annually the LDRRMFIP to include the PPAs chargeable against the unexpended balance of LDDRMF under the Special Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly comply with R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised I.R.R. on the timely posting of Notice of Award, Notice to Proceed and approved Contract in the PhilGEPS website to achieve the objectives of promoting competitiveness and transparency in the procurement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and make necessary adjustment, if necessary, to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data on the Municipality’s financial operations as of December 31, 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain a maintenance/ repair record of each equipment/vehicle owned and/or operated by the Municipal Government.

We also recommended that damaged and unserviceable supplies and properties be kept in a safe stockroom located within the premise of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all department heads to submit at the beginning of the year, the list of all on-going government projects/ programs/activities and those that are to be implemented during the year together with the Consolidated Quarterly Report on Government Projects/Programs/Activities for the quarter ending March 31 to December 31, 2018 and years thereafter to ensure compliance with laws, rules and regulations particularly COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

We also recommended that the department head concerned inform the audit team within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/project/activity that the appropriate signboards and/or public notices are already posted for validation.

We recommended that the officials concerned submit, together with the GAD Accomplishment Report, the documents enumerated above to determine the appropriate amount of the programs/ projects/activities to be attributed to GAD budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties and equipment before losing its value due to rapid deterioration considering the length of time that it had been unserviceable. 


We further recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant adhere to the proper accounting of unserviceable properties with regard to account classification and taking up of depreciation pursuant to Section 140 of the MNGAS for LGU, Volume III, to ensure correct presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
We further recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2017 bonuses amounting to ₱3,504,396.60.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) to support the account for “Other Receivables”, to ensure the validity of the account balance of ₱2,466,834.69 as of December 31, 2016.

We also recommended that OIC – Municipal Accountant maintain and keep subsidiary ledgers for all accounts appearing in the trial balance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive through the Sangguniang Bayan hire qualified personnel to occupy the three (3) Staff positions to assist the LDRRMO following the position titles and salary grades set by Rule 6 Section 2 of IRR of RA No. 10121 and Item 5.2, 5.3 and 5.4 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014 to be able to properly perform their functions and responsibilities as LDRRM Officer and Staff.
We further recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2015 PEI amounting to ₱2,053,659.83.
We recommended that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2014 Productivity Enhancement Incentive amounting to ₱580,000.00.

The OIC - Municipal Accountant should comply with the applicable section of COA Circular No. 97-001 dated February 5, 1997, particularly on the guidelines on dormant funds and request for the write-off of said dormant accounts from the COA Commission Proper.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Inventory Committee to submit the RPCPPE using the format prescribed under the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Volume II.
We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General Services Officer to prepare property cards and to strictly monitor the issuances and transfers of PPE by issuing Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE). Likewise, a reconciliation of the actual physical count of property with the accounting records, and PPELCs with theTrial Balance be undertaken to ensure the reliability of PPE accounts as presented in the financial statements.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-01 dated May 26, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant:
a.	To prepare journal entry to recognize as Property, Plant and Equipment in the General Fund the abovementioned completed project funded through LGSF-SALINTUBIG program; and
b.	Henceforth, to take up as Construction in Progress in the Trust Fund the accumulated cost of the infrastructure project while still under construction pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2015-008 and 2015-009. Upon completion, this account shall be reclassified to the appropriate asset account in the General Fund from the Trust Fund.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-07 dated May 25, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts in retrieving the records of the abovementioned properties and to provide the corresponding depreciation using the determined estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation in compliance to existing government rules and regulations.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-08 dated May 31, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for PPE and to exert effort in tracing, identifying and accounting of projects recorded in the CIP account. Likewise, gather the relevant documents related to the completed projects and prepare journal entries to record the transfer from CIP account to the appropriate PPE accounts.
We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the Municipal Accountant verify the date of completion of the projects and make necessary adjustments relative to the current and prior years unrecorded depreciation expense.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-02 dated May 25, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the Municipal Accountant:
a.	Reconcile with the depository bank for the discrepancies noted between the bank statements and the agency records;
b.	Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to effect the adjustments of the valid reconciling items appearing in the BRS in order to reflect the correct balance of the cash in bank account; and
c.	Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer in sending written notice to the payee of the existence of the check.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-04 dated May 25, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances of their records, Subsidiary Ledgers and Cash Books respectively, to ensure correct and accurate reporting, thereby validating the correctness of the cash accounts.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-09 dated May 31, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned officials and employees to submit the lacking documents in order to further validate the propriety of the disbursements.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-14 dated June 3, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials and employees to:
Implement the prompt and proper liquidation of trust funds received from Source Agencies, including the return of excess/unutilized funds; and
Reconcile the unliquidated fund transfers with the Source Agencies and prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items noted.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-10 dated May 31, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to henceforward record the purchases of construction materials for development projects by administration as inventory and the consumption thereof in the appropriate PPE accounts.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-06 dated May 25, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to properly enforce the withholding of the retention money from suppliers pursuant to the provisions of RA No. 9184 before payment from here on.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-05 dated May 25, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General Services Office to:
a.	Facilitate the LTO registration and insurance of all motor vehicles owned by the Municipality;
b.	Refrain from using unregistered vehicles owned by the Municipality and regularly monitor the expiration of all motor vehicles registration and its timely renewal to avoid invalidation, fines/penalties for delinquency and additional losses; and
c.	Conduct physical inventory of all the Motor Vehicles owned by the Municipality and reconcile the result with the accounting records.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 20202-03 dated May 25, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct concerned officials and employees to refrain from incurring expenditures which are religious in nature and not related to the core functions of the LGU.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-11 dated June 3, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly comply with R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised IRR on the timely posting of notices to achieve the objectives of promoting competitiveness and transparency in the procurement.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-12 dated June 3, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned officials and employees to:
a.	Exert extra care in the post-qualification of the bids in order to properly determine whether the bidder concerned are responsive to all the requirements and conditions specified in the RA No. 9184.
b.	Submit the lacking documents in order to further validate the propriety of the disbursements. Otherwise, the members of the Bids and Awards Committee and TWG submit a justification why the projects were awarded to the winning suppliers/contractors in spite non-submission of the documentary requirements of RA No. 9184.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-13 dated June 3, 2022.

We reiterated our recommendations to the City Mayor to require the:
1) Inventory Committee to conduct the physical inventory of all PPEs
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 and Section 124 of the NGAS
Manual for LGUs, Volume I;
2) CGSO and OCA to reconcile the balances of the RPCPPE with the
property and accounting records;
3) CGSO to initiate the disposal of unserviceable properties in order to drop
these accounts in the books in accordance with existing rules and
regulations; and
4) City Accountant to:
a) Derecognize unserviceable PPEs after disposal;
b) Reclassify Water Supply Systems in the amount of P19,776,019.32
into the appropriate PPE accounts;
c) Provide depreciation on all depreciable assets.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) City Accountant to:
1. take up in the books unrecorded reconciling items like the
interest income and those which have been long outstanding;
2. consistently coordinate with the bank to settle bank debits and
credits unknown to the City and request to send promptly any debit and credit
advice;
3. ensure that all identified reconciling items are analyzed,
documented and recorded in the books to show correct balance of the Cash in Bank accounts; and
4. prepare adjusting journal entries to restore the liabilities of the City to
creditors whose checks became stale and to reflect the correct Cash in Bank
balance, by debiting Cash in Bank account and crediting the Accounts
Payable account.
b) City Treasurer to review the stale checks and secure the addresses of the
payees for notification of the return of stale checks in their
possession for replacement.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to
include a disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements, the restrictions
of the Cash In Bank balances of MCC pertaining ownership rights, and the
reasons why said balances are still made part in the Cash in Bank balance of
the City, as of year end, in compliance with existing accounting and auditing
laws, rules and regulations, to provide adequate information to users of the
Financial Statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
1.) CGS Officer to:
a) prepare SSMI and submit it together with the RIS to the accounting
office as a basis for recording the expense portion of the inventories;
b) maintain and update SCs on all inventories, reconcile the same with
SLCs and existing physical inventory on hand as of a given date, to
validate correctness and existence of inventories; and
c) initiate disposal of obsolete and unserviceable inventories in
accordance with existing rules and regulations on disposal of
inventories/properties.
2.) Inventory Committee to:
a) conduct the physical count of inventories as of June 30 and
December 31, in accordance with regulations, and, prepare the
RPCI and copy furnished the Audit Team;
b) conduct the physical count of unserviceable inventories and prepare
the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property
(IIRUP- Annex 51 of NGAS Manual) in accordance with existing
regulations;
3.) City Accountant to:
a) record all procurement of supplies and materials as Inventories;
b) maintain SLs and corresponding SLCs on all Inventory accounts to
substantiate accuracy and correctness of recorded Inventories;
c) record the incurred expenses of inventories based on the submitted
SSMI and RIS from the CGSO;
d) prepare the related adjusting entries for differences noted in the
reconciliation, if any;
e) record disposal of unserviceable inventories duly supported with the
original copy of the IIRUP as basis for adjusting the accounts
affected.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to record
correctly financial transactions charged to NGAs funds by recognizing the
asset purchased and appropriate expenses as it is incurred; book up
Liquidation Report/Fund Utilization Reports to close the liability account;
and close nominal accounts at year-end, in order to reflect in the books of
accounts accurate financial information.

We recommended further, to use Subsidy from NGAs account instead of
Subsidy from Other Funds when recognizing the income from the
implementation of programs requiring liquidation reports from NGAs.

We recommended that the City Mayor coordinate with the SAs to provide
MOA or its equivalent in order to provide the pertinent information as to the
utilization of the fund, and to require the City Accountant to initiate
preparation of final Fund Utilization Reports and refund unutilized balances
to the SAs.

To ensure that the 20% DF is optimally utilized and monitored, we
recommended that the City Mayor, as Chairman of the City Development
Council, require the:
1. OCA to maintain a separate books of accounts for the 20% DF in
accordance with regulations;
2. The City Budget Officer to render a periodic update to the City Mayor
on the SAAOB of the 20% DF and identify excess funds of completed
projects and recommend the reversion and made available for re-
appropriation;
3. The City Planning and Development Office to ensure the
implementation of PPAs during the year and recommend to the City
Development Council the re-alignment of appropriations of PPAs
Continuing Appropriation no longer identified as priority development
PPAs;
4. The City Engineer’s Office, and other implementing offices concerned,
to monitor the implementation of PPAs of the 20% DF, in order to
achieve the objectives of the fund;
5. City Development Council, to revisit PPAs charged to Continuing
Appropriations, identify balances to be rechanneled to more priority
and relevant PPAs of the City; and ensure that the development
projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned, as well as
procurement- and implementation-ready in order to achieve the
objectives of the fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to
recognize the utilization of the previous years’ unspent DRRM Fund by
debiting to the Trust Liabilities-Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
account in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12,
2012. Henceforth, stop using the Grants and Donations in Cash account in
recording expenditures charged to the previous years unspent DRRM Fund.

Henceforth, the Audit Team recommended that an appropriation ordinance be
enacted by the Sangguniang Panglungsod regarding utilization of prior
years’ unspent balance of DRRM Fund recorded in the Special Trust Fund
books, before government funds are disbursed, to ensure legality and
regularity of the disbursement transactions.

We recommended further that expenditures charged to the unexpended
LDRRM Funds of prior years be included in the LDRRMF Investment Plan
or through Supplemental LDRRMF Investment Plan and incorporated in the
AIP to comply with regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to record
correctly financial transactions on downloaded funds by recognizing first the
liability account upon receipt of the fund; record appropriate expenses as it
is incurred; book up Liquidation Report/Fund Utilization Reports to close
the liability account; and close nominal accounts at year-end, in order to
reflect in the books of accounts accurate financial information.

We also recommended for the immediate submission of the lacking
supporting documents for the proper disposition in audit of the
disbursement. Henceforth, only disbursement vouchers/payrolls with
complete supporting documents should be processed for payment.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
1. City Treasurer to:
a. ensure the received donations in cash should be deposited in the bank
account exclusively maintained for the DRRM Fund;
b. submit to the Accounting Office daily, a separate Report of
Collections and Deposits (RCD) for DRRM, and other supporting
documents such as Official Receipts and deposit slips pertaining to the
donations in cash received, for recording in the books of accounts of the City.
2. City Accountant to:
a. record donations in cash in the Cash Receipts Journal as “ Trust
Liabilities-DRRMF” the donations based on the RCD and supporting
documents submitted by the City Treasurer, or based on the credit memo if the donations are directly deposited by the donor to the
agency bank account;
b. prepare and maintain separate SLs for “ Trust Liabilities-DRRMF”
for cash donations received amounting to ₱100,000.00 and above per
donor per purpose, while those below ₱100,000.00 shall be grouped
and posted in one SL labeled as “Various Donors” per purpose, as
required in Item IV.B.7 of COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April
15, 2014;
c. disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the Trust Liabilities-
DRRMF following the prescribed format.
3. The City General Services Officer to maintain an Inventory Record of the

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor do the following:

a. 	Enjoin the officials concerned to comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, as failure of the Accountable Officer to liquidate his cash advance within the prescribed period shall constitute for the withholding of his salary and the imposition of other sanctions. 

b. 	Direct the Municipal Accountant to investigate the particulars of the long-outstanding unliquidated cash advances amounting to ₱139,830.34 so as to identify the name of the accountable officers and enforce its liquidation/ settlement.  



c. 	Instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile its records so as to determine the unreconciled balances between the aging schedule of unliquidated cash advances and the financial statement balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant  reconcile the cashbook balance with the subsidiary/general ledger balance at least once at the end of each quarter, so that discrepancies will be immediately traced and necessary adjustments be made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the personnel concerned to strictly implement the projects funded by the Iloilo Provincial Government in accordance with the conditions stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement. Request for extension must be seek first from the Iloilo Provincial Government in case of delays in project implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that the provisions of DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 on the use of 20% Development Fund is properly observed so that appropriate utilization and implementation of the developmental projects is carried out effectively and efficiently and to discontinue from charging similar expenses that have no capital expenditure property or components to avoid sanctions such as possible disallowance. 

We likewise recommended that projects/programs with general use or having administrative nature must be appropriated/funded out of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Solid Waste Management Board to revisit the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management (SWM) Plan and implement the same in accordance with Republic Act No. (RA) 9003.



We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other personnel concerned to enforce the closure of the open dumpsite and develop and create a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site, to ensure protection of public health and environment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to exert effort in the collection of tax delinquencies through publication, demand letters and massive tax campaigns in accordance with Sections 254, 255 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160. 

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to avail of the civil remedies for the collection of real property tax, if warranted by circumstances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to regularly remit the amounts due to the BIR, GSIS, PAG-IBIG and Philhealth within the required remittance period to avoid penalties and surcharges pursuant to Section 69 of PD 1445, Section 6(b) of RA No. 8291, RA No. 7875 as amended by RA No. 9241) and RA No. 7742.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the record to facilitate remittance of prior years’ balances and provide a detailed breakdown of the balances of each trust liabilities as of December 31, 2019 so as to identify the period and names of personnel these balances are attributed to.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct periodic reconciliation with the Municipal Treasurer of the Cash in Bank balances per books and per cashbooks.

Notice of Disallowance will be issued for the refund of Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA) paid to the Municipal Accountant for the period August 2017 to March 2018 amounting to ₱96,000.00.

We recommended that the SWM Board:

Enact and enforce laws and/or ordinances related to the implementation of the activities under the plan including imposing penalties/sanctions to any violator. 

Establish a risk management process to identify all possible risks in the implementation of SWM operations. The risks shall be evaluated in order to determine the possible impact in the effective implementation of the ESWMP. 

Develop appropriate treatment plans for the identified risks and determining the persons responsible in executing the plan at the appropriate time. The intended treatment shall as much as possible, avoid the occurrence of the risk and/or manage its effect or impact in the attainment of SWM objective.    Accountant  reconcile the cashbook balance  with  the  subsidiary/general

   to prepare a  reconciliation  report  of  reciprocal  accounts  and  make

   balances and  present  reliable  data  on  the  Municipality’s  financial

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: 

Prepare and submit monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on time in order to check the accuracy of the balances of the bank accounts and to submit the journal entry vouchers to ensure proper recording of all valid reconciling items that require adjustment/correction in the GL. 

Make a formal request to the depository bank for the release of bank statements with corresponding debit/credit advices within the prescribed period as per regulations.

Conduct periodic reconciliation with the Municipal Treasurer of the Cash in Bank balances per books and per cashbooks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all officials and employees concerned comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, as failure of the Accountable Officer to liquidate his cash advance within the prescribed period shall constitute for the withholding of his salary and the imposition of other sanctions. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant should furnish the Municipal Mayor as well as the department heads and division chiefs, a listing of long-outstanding unliquidated cash advances of employees under their charge to enforce liquidation/ settlement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other officials concerned see to it that the provisions of DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 on the use of 20% Development Fund is properly observed so that appropriate utilization and implementation of the developmental projects will be carried out effectively and efficiently and to discontinue from charging similar expenses that have no capital expenditure property or components to avoid sanctions such as possible disallowance. 

We likewise recommended that projects/programs with general use or having administrative nature must be appropriated/funded out of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the MDRRM Officer to prepare and submit each month a Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the audit team on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council. Provisions of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 should be strictly followed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the designated GSO and the MDRRMO to conduct regular inventory of MDRRM supplies and equipment available at its disposal and to update its records to aid the concerned officials of the municipality in its decision-making process in conformity with Section VI of COA Circular No. 2014-002.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide a more accessible storage room where the DRRM supplies and equipment will be kept for easy retrieval during emergency cases.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to record the financial transactions daily, pursuant to Sections 26, 27, and 28 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and to update and balance all the transactions and reconcile the same with the cash on hand as provided in Section 63 (D) of Local Treasury Operations Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all department heads to submit at the beginning of the year, the list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented during the year together with the Consolidated Quarterly Report on Government Projects/Programs/ Activities for the quarter ending March 31 to December 31, 2018 and years thereafter to ensure compliance with laws, rules and regulations particularly COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

We also recommended that the department head concerned inform the audit team within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/project/activity that the appropriate signboards and/or public notices are already posted for validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to deposit the full amount of collections not later than the next banking day to the authorized depository bank and to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445, Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGU, Vol. 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the employees concerned revisit the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 103 and Section 55 of the General Provisions of GAA of FY 2018 on the grant of RATA to avoid incurrence of the same violation in the future.

Notice of disallowance will be issued for the refund of Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA) paid to the Municipal Accountant for the period August 2017 to March 2018 amounting to ₱96,000.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to submit immediately the lacking disbursement vouchers and supporting documents. 

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure strict compliance with the aforesaid laws, rules and regulations and closely monitor the submission of reports and supporting documents by the accountable officers for immediate recording in the books of accounts so as to facilitate submission thereof to the auditor within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct all department heads to discontinue the practice of sending job order personnel to attend team building activities, trainings, seminars and workshops to minimize incurrence of additional operating expenses.

We also recommended strict compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations relative to job order personnel to avoid possible disallowances or suspensions of transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and officials concerned perform the following:

(a) Require the contractor to engage in the catch-up program in order to fast track the completion of the project to avoid rescindment of contract as stipulated in Section 68 of RA 9184. 

(b)	Conduct an inventory of work accomplished by the contractor and impose liquidated damages at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay. 

(c) 	Intensify monitoring of project in order to avoid recurrence of delay. 

(d) Adhere to the regulations on the posting of signboard in the project location as soon as the award has been made in order to inform the public, in the case of future similar project.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to regularly remit the amounts due to the BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth within the required remittance period pursuant to Section 69 of PD 1445, Section 6(b) of RA No. 8291, RA No. 7875 as amended by RA No. 9241) and RA No. 7742 to avoid penalties and surcharges.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile record to facilitate remittance of prior year’s balances and provide a detailed breakdown of the balances of each trust liabilities as of December 31, 2018, so as to identify the period and names of personnel these balances are attributed to.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Treasurer  to expedite the release of the Real Property Tax shares to the respective barangays pursuant to Section 271(d) of RA 7160 so that the barangays could utilize the amount for whatever projects or proper operating expenditures they may incur for the benefit of its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all officials concerned to ensure the maximum utilization of the 20% development fund so that infrastructure projects are efficiently and effectively implemented during the year to provide socio-economic development for the upliftment of the lives of its constituents. Moreover, the municipal government should strengthen its monitoring of the projects so that appropriate information on the status be given to the public and other stakeholders.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) to support the account for “Other Receivables”, to ensure the validity of the account balance of ₱2,466,834.69 as of December 31, 2016.

We also recommended that OIC – Municipal Accountant maintain and keep subsidiary ledgers for all accounts appearing in the trial balance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive through the Sangguniang Bayan hire qualified personnel to occupy the three (3) Staff positions to assist the LDRRMO following the position titles and salary grades set by Rule 6 Section 2 of IRR of RA No. 10121 and Item 5.2, 5.3 and 5.4 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014 to be able to properly perform their functions and responsibilities as LDRRM Officer and Staff.

We recommended that the LDRRMO adhere strictly with the provisions of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 so that the proper utilization of the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund will be verified as to correctness in a timely manner by the auditor.
We further recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2016 PEI amounting to ₱562,000.00.

We recommended that the SWM Board:

Formulate and document systems, processes and procedures to serve as a guide by SWMO in the implementation of the ESWMP and its current SWM function. 

Enact and enforce laws and/or ordinances related to the implementation of the activities under the plan including imposing penalties/sanctions to any violator. 

Establish a risk management process to identify all possible risks in the implementation of SWM operations. The risks shall be evaluated in order to determine the possible impact in the effective implementation of the ESWMP. 
Develop appropriate treatment plans for the identified risks and determining the persons responsible in executing the plan at the appropriate time. The intended treatment shall as much as possible, avoid the occurrence of the risk and/or manage its effect or impact in the attainment of SWM objective. There shall also be periodic monitoring and review of the risk plan to ensure its relevance with the current trend in SWM operation. 

Adopt revenue generating measures pursuant to Section 12 (4) of Republic Act 9003 in order to ensure the viability of its 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.

Revisit the provisions of Republic Act 9003 dated January 26, 2001 and comply the guidelines of Rule 13 Section 2 of the aforementioned law.
We further recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2015 PEI amounting to ₱2,053,659.83.
We recommended that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2014 Productivity Enhancement Incentive amounting to ₱580,000.00.
We recommended that management cause the refund of the 2013 productivity enhancement incentive at ₱12,000.00 for each employee.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of properties and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to obtain copies of the bank statements for the time deposits and prepare Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) and effect the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the true balance of the account.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct amount of the remaining balance of the City’s loan payables.
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to update the accounting records and reconciled to reflect the correct cash balance of LDRRMF as of year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC Chairman, City Engineer, CPDC, and City Agricultural Officer to fast-track the implementation of priority programs, projects and activities to provide constituents of the benefits due them.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct all the Department Heads assigned to spearhead the implementation of projects charged against the 20% DF to exhaust remedies to fully implement these projects in a timely manner.

We recommended that the BAC and its TWG be more circumspect in the evaluation of bids and to examine each proposal whether it meets the minimum requirements prescribed for goods or projects under procurement.

We recommended that the City Mayor (a) require the City Treasurer to encourage the defaulting stallholders to enter into Compromise Agreement in order to afford them the chance to settle their dues and continue with their source of living pursuant to Section 16 of the Market Code; (b) cause the revocation of the Business Permit and order the stoppage of its operation thru closure of stalls of delinquent stallholders who refuse to submit to a Compromise Agreement in consonance with Section 18 of the Market Code; (c) should the first and second recommendation fail, institute administrative and judicial remedies in order to fully collect the amount due to the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to establish a system of collection to enforce the collection of market stall rental fee and demand for the collection of unpaid rent; and the City Accountant to maintain individual ledger card of the market stall lessee for easy monitoring and enforcement of collection and strict implementation of the provisions of the contract of lease.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to demand for the liquidation of cash advances from the accountable persons concerned.

We recommended that the City Mayor designate alternate BAC members to ensure continuity of the procurement process or the creation of a separate BAC for the procurement of Goods and Infrastructure. This will result in lower volume of procurement to be undertaken by each BAC and faster processing; b) establish procurement timelines in consonance with project schedules, including a cut-off date for processing requisitions and issuance of purchase orders to ensure receipt of most of the procured items in time for the project implementation, with provisions for exceptions in case of emergencies.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and Treasurer to prepare the Disbursement Voucher in all loan installment payments. Supporting documents such as the ADA and bank statement should also be attached as supporting documents to the Disbursement Voucher.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Human Resource Management Officer to revisit the provisions of Section 77 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160. Further, we also recommended the filling-up of vacant positions per plantilla items/position of the City to handle works that require career regular employees.
We recommended that the City Mayor compel the GAD Focal Person to submit justification on the incurrence of unauthorized GAD expenditures.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to submit the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report to the COA Audit Team within the period prescribed by regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CSWD to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-programs and projects in order to attain the objective for which funds were provided.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Solid Waste Management Unit (SWMU) to develop a well-designed, current and recalibrated community awareness program and choose the appropriate means of communication for it to be effective.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Solid Waste Management Program Focal Person to maintain disposal site records in the Residual Containment Area (RCA).

We recommended that the City Mayor require the SWMU to establish a mechanism to measure the volume and type of waste to be placed at the site, which will provide baseline data to assist in developing effective waste minimization strategies and future plans for waste management.

We recommended that the City Mayor cause the allocation of funds to purchase the appropriate safety clothing as prescribed in the Manual for Operation and Maintenance at Sanitary Landfill; the SWMU should ensure that any person entering the dumpsite must wear the proper safety clothing so that health risks are minimized.

We recommended that the RCA be fenced with a secured gate at the entrance to minimize illegal visitors or scavengers. This will also help address the occupational health and safety issues by limiting access.

We recommended that a proper gate at the entrance to the RCA must be built so that it is easier for the personnel at the site to stop vehicles coming inside at the gate; inspect the waste to confirm the type of waste to be disposed then direct the vehicle to the designated area depending on the type of waste already inspected. This daily process will compensate for the non-segregation of waste at source and will ensure that wastes being disposed at the RCA are properly segregated. We further recommended that the SWMU should implement a color coding or appropriate means of identification to ensure that wastes are properly segregated at the RCA.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to enforce the immediate settlement of audit disallowances and to ensure that employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies should first settle the disallowances and charges for which they are liable in accordance with Section 7 of COA Circular No 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to reconcile their records with that of the GSIS.

Order No. 2010-003, 2016-0029 and 2018-0001; and
b. apply for a DOH License to Operate for the remaining three ambulance units,
including the five other units which are due for renewal this year 2020.

Services to:
a. Secure from the GSIS-GIF an insurance on all buildings of the city

b. Prepare the PIF as required under Paragraph 5.1.e of COA Circular 2018-
002;
c. Submit the consolidated PIF to the Audit Team leader per Paragraph 5.1.e

2016-005 supported with an aging of dormant receivables and fund transfers,
indicating in the remarks column the existence of the applicable conditions, as
follows:
a. Absence of records or documents to validate/support the claim and/or
unreconciled reciprocal accounts
b. Death of the accountable officer/employee/debtor
c. Unknown whereabouts of the accountable officer/employee/debtor, and
that he/she could not be located despite diligent efforts to find him/her
d. Exhaustion of all possible remedies by the Management to collect the
receivables and to demand liquidation of cash advances and fund
transfers
e. No pending case in court involving the subject dormant accounts.

a. require the GAD FPS to adhere to the provisions of PCW-NEDA-DBM-DILG
JMC Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01 and ensure that: (a) the PPAs included in

d) reconcile its records and remit immediately the unremitted amounts

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an inventory committee  to
   conduct actual physical count of property, plant and equipment and submit
   the RPCPPE, in conformity with Section 124 and Annex 49 of the Manual  on
   the New Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume  I  and  II,
   respectively.  Likewise, a reconciliation of the actual physical count of
   property  with  the  accounting  records  be  undertaken  to  ensure  the
   reliability of PPE accounts as presented in the financial statements.


   We further recommended that the Acting Supply Officer/Municipal Treasurer
   and the Municipal Accountant prepare the property cards and ledger  cards
   and to reconcile the postings/records of their PPE thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant
   to:


     1. Prepare and submit monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on time in
        order to check the accuracy of the balances of the bank accounts and
        to submit the journal entry vouchers to ensure proper  recording  of
        all valid reconciling items that  require  adjustment/correction  in
        the GL.


     2. Make a formal request to the depository bank for the release of bank
        statements  with  corresponding  debit/credit  advices  within   the
        prescribed period as per regulations.


     3. Conduct periodic reconciliation with the Municipal Treasurer of  the
        Cash in Bank balances per books and per cashbooks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all officials  and  employees
   concerned comply with the provisions  of  COA  Circular  No.  97-002,  as
   failure of the Accountable Officer to liquidate his cash  advance  within
   the prescribed period shall constitute for the withholding of his  salary
   and the imposition of other sanctions.

   We also recommended that the  Municipal  Accountant  should  furnish  the
   Municipal Mayor as well as the department heads and  division  chiefs,  a
   listing of long-outstanding unliquidated cash advances of employees under
   their charge to enforce liquidation/ settlement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal  Accountant
   to prepare a  reconciliation  report  of  reciprocal  accounts  and  make
   necessary adjustment,  if  necessary,  to  reflect  the  correct  account
   balances and  present  reliable  data  on  the  municipality’s  financial
   operation as of December 31, 2018.

   We further recommended that the Municipal  Accountant  regularly  perform
   the review and appraisal of transactions to avoid error and omissions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other officials concerned see
   to it that the provisions of DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC)
   No. 2017-1 on the use of 20% Development Fund  is  properly  observed  so
   that appropriate utilization  and  implementation  of  the  developmental
   projects  will  be  carried  out  effectively  and  efficiently  and   to
   discontinue  from  charging  similar  expenses  that  have   no   capital
   expenditure property or components to avoid sanctions  such  as  possible
   disallowance.

   We likewise recommended that projects/programs with general use or having
   administrative nature must be  appropriated/funded  out  of  the  General
   Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the  Municipal  Accountant
   and the MDRRM Officer to prepare  and  submit  each  month  a  Report  on
   Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the audit team on or before the  15th
   day after the end of each month,  through  the  Municipal  Disaster  Risk
   Reduction Management  Council  and  the  Municipal  Development  Council.
   Provisions of Item 5.1.5 of COA  Circular  2012-002  should  be  strictly
   followed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the designated  GSO  and
   the MDRRMO to conduct regular inventory of MDRRM supplies  and  equipment
   available at its disposal and to update its records to aid the  concerned
   officials  of  the  municipality  in  its  decision-making   process   in
   conformity with Section VI of COA Circular No. 2014-002.

   We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide a more accessible
   storage room where the DRRM supplies and equipment will be kept for  easy
   retrieval during emergency cases.

We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor  require  the  Acting  Municipal
   Treasurer  to  record  the  financial  transactions  daily,  pursuant  to
   Sections 26, 27, and 28 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II  and  to  update
   and balance all the transactions and reconcile the same with the cash  on
   hand as provided in Section 63 (D) of Local Treasury Operations Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all department  heads  to
   submit at the beginning of the year, the list of all on-going  government
   projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented  during
   the year together with the Consolidated Quarterly  Report  on  Government
   Projects/Programs/Activities for the quarter ending March 31 to  December
   31, 2018 and years thereafter to ensure compliance with laws,  rules  and
   regulations particularly COA Circular  No.  2013-004  dated  January  30,
   2013.


   We also recommended that the department head concerned inform  the  audit
   team within ten (10) days after the award of the  infrastructure  project
   or before the start of the program/project/activity that the  appropriate
   signboards and/or public notices are already posted for validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  instruct  the  Acting  Municipal
   Treasurer to deposit the full amount of collections not  later  than  the
   next banking day to the authorized depository bank and to strictly adhere
   to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445, Section 28  of  COA  Circular
   No. 92-382 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGU, Vol. 1.

We recommended that the  Municipal  Mayor  and  the  employees  concerned
   revisit the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 103  and  Section
   55 of the General Provisions of GAA of FY 2018 on the grant  of  RATA  to
   avoid incurrence of the same violation in the future.

   Notice of disallowance will be issued for the  refund  of  Representation
   and Transportation Allowance (RATA) paid to the Municipal Accountant  for
   the period August 2017 to March 2018 amounting to ₱96,000.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer,
   Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to submit  immediately
   the lacking disbursement vouchers and supporting documents.

   We likewise recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  and  Municipal
   Treasurer ensure strict compliance with the  aforesaid  laws,  rules  and
   regulations and closely monitor the submission of reports and  supporting
   documents by the accountable officers  for  immediate  recording  in  the
   books of accounts so as to facilitate submission thereof to  the  auditor
   within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that the  Municipal  Mayor  direct  the  Bids  and  Awards
   Committee to furnish the Auditor with copies of all perfected  contracts,
   purchase orders and the like, and all supporting documents  forming  part
   thereof for reference or incorporation therein within  five  (5)  working
   days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase  orders
   in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001, so that review,  inspection
   and evaluation could be made accordingly.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct all  department  heads  to
   discontinue the practice of sending job order personnel  to  attend  team
   building  activities,  trainings,  seminars  and  workshops  to  minimize
   incurrence of additional operating expenses.

   We also recommend strict  compliance  with  applicable  laws,  rules  and
   regulations  relative  to  job  order   personnel   to   avoid   possible
   disallowances or suspensions of transactions.

(a) Require the contractor to engage in the catch-up program in order  to
      fast track the completion of  the  project  to  avoid  rescindment  of
      contract as stipulated in Section 68 of RA 9184.

   (b)      Conduct an inventory of work accomplished by the contractor  and
      impose liquidated damages at least equal to one-tenth of  one  percent
      (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay.

   (c)      Intensify monitoring of project in order to avoid recurrence  of
      delay.

   (d) Adhere to the regulations on the posting of signboard in the  project
      location as soon as the award has been made in  order  to  inform  the
      public, in the case of future similar project.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal  Treasurer
   to regularly remit the  amounts  due  to  the  BIR,  GSIS,  PAG-IBIG  and
   Philhealth within the required remittance period pursuant to  Section  69
   of PD 1445, Section 6(b) of RA 8291, RA 7875 as amended by RA  9241)  and
   RA 7742 to avoid penalties and surcharges.

   We also recommended that the Municipal  Accountant  reconcile  record  to
   facilitate remittance of prior year’s balances  and  provide  a  detailed
   breakdown of the balances of each trust liabilities as  of  December  31,
   2018, so as to identify the period and names of personnel these  balances
   are attributed to.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  instruct  the  Solid  Waste
   Management Board to revisit the  Municipality’s  Solid  Waste  Management
   (SWM) Plan and implement the same in accordance  with  Republic  Act  No.
   (RA) 9003.

   We  also  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  and  other  personnel
   concerned to enforce the closure of the open  dumpsite  and  develop  and
   create a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site, to ensure protection
   of public health and environment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the officials  and  employees
   concerned comply with the provisions  of  the  Philippine  Commission  on
   Women (PCW), Department of the  Interior  and  Local  Government  (DILG),
   Department of Budget and  Management  (DBM)  and  National  Economic  and
   Development Authority (NEDA) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No.  2013-01
   re: Guidelines on the Localization  of  the  Magna  Carta  of  Women,  as
   amended by Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2016-01.

   We further recommended that the officials concerned submit, together with
   the  GAD  Accomplishment  Report,  the  documents  enumerated  above   to
   determine the appropriate amount of the  programs/projects/activities  to
   be attributed to GAD budget.
