8.15 The awarding of a contract to a bidder with unresponsive bid was relayed to Management
through AOM No. 2018-01 dated February 8, 2019.

8.16 During the exit conference, the BAC Chairman explained that the Program of Work was
revised due to an error in the computation of steel works. The revision was already
approved by the City Mayor before the Invitation to Bid was posted. Hence, no Bid
Bulletin was issued for the purpose. This is also the reason why the bid proposal
submitted by JQD did not include the paintworks. As for the bid offer of Juncynth
Construction, the BAC Chairman has no idea why it included paintworks when the bid
documents pertaining to the scope of works were already revised. The BAC also added
that it did not recommend the award of the contract to Juncynth because it did not rely on
the strength of the structure of the facility that Juncynth would build as it does not include
the revision made on the project. Also, the City Engineer pointed out that since the
building is an Evacuation Facility, the strength of the structure is of paramount concern.
That is why they are wary of giving the contract to Juncynth Construction.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:
However, a revisit on the bid proposals of both JQD Construction and Juncynth
construction would reveal that they contain the same scopes of work. The volume and
quantity of materials needed for the steelworks are the same. Hence, there is no good
basis to conclude that the bid proposal of JQD is better than that of Juncynth.
Based on the documents submitted, including the answer submitted by the BAC, it
appears that Juncynth Construction has a better bid proposal than JQD Construction. This
is because it included paint works which is worth ₱ 107,738.51. Although JQD
Construction’s total bid was lower by ₱ 2,089.05, its scope of work did not include the
paintworks. Therefore, it is the Audit Team’s opinion that the contract should have been
awarded to Juncynth, which offered a better term for the project.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the SWM Board:

Formulate and document systems, processes and procedures to serve as a guide by SWMO in the implementation of the ESWMP and its current SWM function. 

Enact and enforceg laws and/or ordinances related to the implementation of the activities under the plan including imposing penalties/sanctions to any violator. 


Establish a risk management process to identify all possible risks in the implementation of SWM operations. The risks shall be evaluated in order to determine the possible impact in the effective implementation of the ESWMP. 

Develop appropriate treatment plans for the identified risks and determining the persons responsible in executing the plan at the appropriate time. The intended treatment shall as much as possible, avoid the occurrence of the risk and/or manage its effect or impact in the attainment of SWM objective. There shall also be periodic monitoring and review of the risk plan to ensure its relevance with the current trend in SWM operation. 

Adopt revenue generating measures pursuant to Section 12 (4) of Republic Act 9003 in order to ensure the viability of its 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.

Revisit the provisions of Republic Act 9003 dated January 26, 2001 and comply the guidelines of  Rule 13 Section 2 of the aforementioned law.

Recommendation

      We recommended that the inventory committee:


   1. Summarize the result of the physical inventory count per  PPE  account
      to come up with the complete list of PPEs;


   2. Reconcile the physical count with the records maintained by the Supply
      Officer/Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant;

   3. Submit the RPCPPE containing the complete list of PPEs with  technical
      descriptions and specifications in conformity with the  provisions  of
      Chapter 7 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGU, Volume I.

   We further recommended that  the  Municipal  Accountant  and  the  Supply
   Officer/Municipal Treasurer undertake immediate reconciliation of the PPE
   per physical inventory conducted as against the property ledger cards and
   property cards and effect adjustments, if any,  to  reflect  the  correct
   balances of PPE accounts as reflected in the financial statements.


   The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management  thru
   Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017-011 dated February 14, 2017.

   MNGAS for LGU, Volume III, to ensure correct presentation of accounts  in

   Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017-017 dated February 14, 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned personnel (Project in Charge, Department Heads, BAC and BAC Secretariat) to strictly observe Rule II, Section 7 of RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 76-41 as well as the imposition of control measures to ensure that procurement is effected in an efficient, economical and effective manner that is most advantageous to the government and to prevent audit disallowances for such violation.
We recommended that the PMOs/end-users prepare PPMP and APP as required by RA 9184 to facilitate validation of the Accomplishment Report.

We recommended that the BAC Secretariat / GSO Head submit to the Auditor a copy of all contracts, purchase orders and the like and each of the supporting documents forming part thereof within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders. We also recommended that within 24 hours from acceptance of the goods or services being delivered, the official responsible for accepting the goods delivered should notify the Auditor of the time and date of the scheduled deliveries. In case of partial delivery of any item in the contract, the copy to be submitted shall indicate which items have been delivered and which were not. Further, we recommended that a copy of the report of inspection or its equivalent be submitted to the Auditor within twenty-four (24) hours from acceptance of the items delivered and, in the case of unserviceable and disposable property/assets, immediately after the inspection thereof by management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to effect the minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay as prescribed under the GAA of 2014 be observed effective January 2015 and onwards.

We recommended that Management refrain from incurring additional PS expenses/cost until such time that the limitation is observed. The implementation of any increase in PS cost should be held in abeyance until the PS limitation/cap of the Municipality is complied with. We recommended that management cause the refund of the 2013 productivity enhancement incentive at P12,000.00 for each employees.

The OIC Municipal Accountant should comply with the applicable section of COA Circular No. 97-001 dated February 5, 1997, particularly on the guidelines on dormant funds and request for the write-off of said dormant accounts from the COA Commission Proper.

We recommended that- 1. There should be a continuous coordination and process of inventory taking between the GSO and all the department/offices of the city to expedite completion of every annual inventory taking and report preparation for a timely reconciliation with the PPE records.

We recommended that the project engineers be required to perform regular and thorough monitoring and supervision of projects to ensure that construction works are accomplished in accordance with the terms of the contract, and that satisfactorily completed projects are paid. Also, impose liquidated damages on the defaulting contractors.

We recommended that the LGU comply with all the requirement of RA 9184, its implementing rules, laws, and related issuances. Specifically, a certification that the detailed engineering surveys and designs have been conducted according to the prescribed agency standards and specifications in conformance with the provisions of this Annex should be attached in the contract and that the detailed engineering outputs are adequate for the procurement at hand.

We recommended that the City Engineer in coordination with the LGU’s Legal Office review and revise the infrastructure contracts to include the required provision so that subsequent contracts which will be entered into by the city government would comply with the relevant requirements of RA 9184 IRR and related laws. The significant terms required by the law should be incorporated in the contract.

We recommended that the BAC carefully observe the proper identification of the contract to be bid and the categorization of the items (goods & services) in the Invitation to Bid/Expression of Interest in posting at the PhilGEPS as well as in the bulletin boards. Likewise, the BAC should submit justification on all the deficiencies discussed herein and why the same should not be suspended/disallowed in audit.
